coaching.title: Today's Run
coaching.message: You're 65 days out and in taper—this is not the day to prove fitness. Do 8–10 miles of rolling terrain at Z2 (140–150 bpm based on your recent work), with 4–6 short (90–120 sec) hill repeats at race-pace effort embedded mid-run. Your last three runs averaged 125–140 bpm; today you need to feel sharp without accumulating fatigue. Recovery score is 84, so you can handle the stimulus, but keep the total volume tight and the easy miles genuinely easy. This is maintenance work—you've already built the engine.

coaching.title: Fuel Right
coaching.message: You slept nearly 9 hours last night with excellent efficiency; that's a sign your body is well-fueled and recovering well. Eat normal breakfast pre-run (carbs + protein, 60–90 min before), and on an 8–10 mile run you don't need to carry fuel unless you're working above Z3—you're not. Post-run, get 30–40g protein and 80–100g carbs within 90 minutes to lock in the training stimulus. With 65 days to race and your corporate travel schedule, consistency matters more than perfection here.

coaching.title: Recovery
coaching.message: Your sleep numbers are excellent—8.98h at 95% efficiency is elite for a 40-something executive. Resting HR at 62 bpm is stable and expected. Keep the overnight routine locked: same bedtime, no screens 30 min before sleep, cool room (65–68°F if possible). During taper, sleep becomes a performance tool; you're in the right place. Monitor for any spike in RHR above 64–65 bpm; if you see it, that signals underrecovery and you cut tomorrow's run.

coaching.title: Mental Edge
coaching.message: You're in week 25 of a 22-week plan—you're past the peak and now managing fatigue and confidence in parallel. The Julian Alps 50K will demand alpine hiking, sparse aid, and 2,500m of vert over 6–7+ hours. Today's hill repeats are small doses of that demand; use them to rehearse your pace, breathing, and self-talk on steep ground. Don't race these repeats; execute them clean. In 65 days, your fitness is set—taper is about arriving fresh and sharp, not desperate.
